title: Quick check, encrypted SIP and SRTP

questions.prompt: Which of these can the analyst still read from a SRTP capture without any decryption keys? (Select all that apply.)
choices: RTP sequence number, timestamp, and SSRC
Loss and jitter computed from RTP headers
The contents of the SDP `a=crypto:` line
questions.explanation: SRTP encrypts the payload but leaves the RTP header in cleartext. Sequence number, timestamp, SSRC, and payload type are visible; Wireshark computes loss and jitter from those headers. Audio samples are encrypted. The SDP `a=crypto:` line is in the SIP body, which is only readable when SIP is plaintext or has been decrypted separately.

questions.prompt: Captured DTLS-SRTP traffic can usually be decrypted after the fact by extracting keys from the SDP body of the corresponding SIP messages.
questions.explanation: DTLS-SRTP keys are derived during the in-band DTLS handshake and never appear in SDP. SDP only carries `a=setup:` and `a=fingerprint:` for verification; the actual session keys exist only in the two endpoints' memory after the handshake. SDES, by contrast, does carry keys in `a=crypto:` SDP attributes.
